Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.overview: Bacterial spot is one of the most devastating diseases of pepper. It causes dark, water-soaked spots on leaves, stems and fruits, leading to significant yield loss especially in warm, wet conditions.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves (1-5mm)
Spots turn brown to black with yellow halos
Leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Dark, raised scabs on pepper fruits
Stem lesions causing wilting
Spots may have a greasy appearance when wet
causes: Bacteria spread through infected seeds
Splashing rain or overhead irrigation spreads bacteria
Warm temperatures (24-30°C) favor spread
High humidity above 80% accelerates infection
Wounds from insects or hail provide entry points
Using infected transplants

immediate_action: Remove and destroy heavily infected plant parts
Avoid working with plants when wet

chemical_treatments.name: Copper Hydroxide
chemical_treatments.timing: Apply in early morning or evening

chemical_treatments.name: Copper Oxychloride
chemical_treatments.notes: Combine with mancozeb for better results

chemical_treatments.name: Streptomycin Sulfate
chemical_treatments.notes: Antibiotic — use sparingly to prevent resistance

organic_treatments.name: Neem Oil Spray
organic_treatments.notes: Add few drops of dish soap as emulsifier

prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds
Apply 2-year crop rotation (avoid Solanaceae family)
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead sprinklers
Space plants properly for good air circulation (45-60cm apart)
Apply preventive copper sprays before rainy season
Remove crop debris after harvest
Avoid touching plants when wet

treatment_timeline.day_1: Remove infected leaves, stop overhead watering, apply copper spray
treatment_timeline.day_3_7: Monitor spread, repeat copper spray if rain occurs
treatment_timeline.week_3_4: If improving, extend spray interval to 10 days
treatment_timeline.month_2: Focus on prevention, maintain spray program

Pepper__bell___healthy.overview: Your pepper plant appears healthy! Continue good agricultural practices to maintain plant health and maximize yield.
prevention: Water at soil level, avoid wetting foliage
Fertilize regularly with balanced NPK fertilizer
Monitor weekly for early signs of disease
Maintain proper plant spacing for airflow
Remove weeds that harbor pests and diseases
maintenance_tips: Mulch around base to retain moisture
Support heavy branches to prevent breakage
Inspect undersides of leaves weekly for pests

treatment_timeline.ongoing: Continue regular monitoring and preventive care

Potato___Early_blight.overview: Early blight is a common fungal disease that appears as dark spots with concentric rings (target board pattern) on older leaves first. It rarely kills plants but significantly reduces yield and tuber quality.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (bulls-eye pattern)
Spots surrounded by yellow tissue
Lower/older leaves affected first
Spots enlarge and merge causing leaf death
Stems show dark elongated lesions
Tubers show dark, sunken, dry lesions
causes: Alternaria solani fungus overwinters in soil and debris
Spores spread by wind and splashing water
Warm days (24-29°C) and cool nights favor disease
Stressful conditions (drought, low fertility) increase susceptibility
Plants weakened by other issues more vulnerable

immediate_action: Do not compost infected material — burn or discard

chemical_treatments.name: Chlorothalonil
chemical_treatments.timing: Start before symptoms appear in high-risk periods
chemical_treatments.notes: Excellent protectant fungicide for early blight

chemical_treatments.name: Mancozeb
chemical_treatments.notes: Broad spectrum protectant, good for prevention

chemical_treatments.name: Azoxystrobin
chemical_treatments.notes: Systemic fungicide — works from inside plant

prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Apply 3-year crop rotation
Plant resistant varieties (Atlantic, Kennebec)
Ensure adequate potassium and calcium fertilization
Apply preventive fungicide at vine closure
Remove volunteer potato plants
Destroy crop residue after harvest

treatment_timeline.day_1: Remove infected lower leaves, apply Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb
treatment_timeline.week_2: Continue spray program, check new growth

Potato___Late_blight.overview: CRITICAL DISEASE. Late blight caused the Irish Potato Famine (1845-49) killing over 1 million people. It can destroy an entire crop in 7-10 days under favorable conditions. Immediate action is essential.
symptoms: Water-soaked pale green spots on leaf edges/tips
Spots rapidly turn brown-black
White fuzzy growth on leaf underside in humid conditions
Dark brown-black stem lesions
Entire plant can collapse within days
Tubers show reddish-brown internal rot
causes: Phytophthora infestans — highly aggressive water mold
Spreads extremely fast in cool (10-20°C) wet conditions
Spores travel miles by wind
Infected seed tubers are primary source
Survives in infected tubers in soil

severity_details.spread_speed: very fast — entire field in 7-10 days

immediate_action: ACT WITHIN 24 HOURS — this disease spreads extremely fast
Alert neighboring farmers — spores spread by wind

chemical_treatments.name: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb
chemical_treatments.notes: Most effective systemic treatment for late blight

chemical_treatments.name: Cymoxanil + Mancozeb
chemical_treatments.timing: Apply in early morning or evening
chemical_treatments.notes: Works curatively up to 3 days after infection

organic_treatments.name: Copper Hydroxide
organic_treatments.notes: Best organic option but less effective than synthetic

prevention: Plant only certified disease-free seed potatoes
Grow resistant varieties (Sarpo Mira, Defender)
Apply preventive fungicide before disease season
Monitor weather forecasts — spray before rain
Use blight forecasting tools (BlightWatch, Smith Period)
Ensure 75cm row spacing for air circulation
Destroy all volunteer potato plants

treatment_timeline.day_2_3: Monitor spread, reapply if rain washes off spray
treatment_timeline.severe_case: Consider destroying crop to protect neighbors

favorable_conditions.risk_period: Smith Period: 2+ days with temp >10°C and humidity >90%

Potato___healthy.overview: Your potato plant looks healthy! Potatoes are vulnerable to several serious diseases — maintain good practices to protect your crop.
prevention: Apply preventive fungicide program during high-risk periods
Monitor weather — spray before rain events
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Hill soil around stems to protect tubers
Scout fields twice weekly during growing season
maintenance_tips: Side dress with nitrogen at vine closure
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle weekly

treatment_timeline.ongoing: Preventive fungicide every 14 days during wet season

Tomato_Bacterial_spot.overview: Bacterial spot causes small dark spots on leaves, stems, and fruits. It is particularly damaging to fruit quality and marketability, with infected fruits being unsaleable.
symptoms: Small water-soaked spots on leaves (1-3mm)
Spots dry to dark brown with yellow margins
Raised scab-like spots on green fruits
Severely infected leaves drop early
Stem streaking in severe cases
causes: Bacteria spread through infected seed
Splashing water spreads infection
Temperature 25-30°C with high humidity
Wind-driven rain during storms

chemical_treatments.name: Copper Oxychloride + Mancozeb
chemical_treatments.notes: Combined protection against bacterial and fungal

prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds or transplants
2-year crop rotation
Use drip irrigation
Apply copper spray preventively at transplanting
Avoid working in field when plants are wet

treatment_timeline.day_1: Remove infected material, apply copper spray

Tomato_Early_blight.overview: Early blight is one of the most common tomato diseases worldwide. It causes characteristic bulls-eye patterned spots and can severely reduce yield if not managed. Most damaging during warm, humid weather.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern)
Yellow halo surrounds each spot
Lower leaves affected first
Leaves turn yellow and drop
Dark collar rot at stem base in seedlings
Sunken dark spots on fruits near stem
causes: Alternaria solani fungus in soil and plant debris
Spores airborne and water-splashed
Warm temperatures (24-29°C)
Alternate wet and dry periods
Plants stressed by drought or nutrient deficiency

immediate_action: Remove all infected lower leaves immediately
Do not compost — dispose of away from field
Apply fungicide within 24 hours of detection

chemical_treatments.name: Chlorothalonil
chemical_treatments.timing: Apply in cool part of day
chemical_treatments.notes: Best broad-spectrum protectant for early blight

chemical_treatments.name: Mancozeb
chemical_treatments.notes: Rotate with other fungicides to prevent resistance

organic_treatments.name: Bacillus subtilis
organic_treatments.notes: Biological control — safe for bees and environment

prevention: Use resistant varieties (Mountain Magic, Iron Lady)
Apply 2-3 year crop rotation
Mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves
Stake plants to improve air circulation
Fertilize adequately — stressed plants more susceptible
Water at base of plants, not overhead
Apply preventive fungicide at first flowering

favorable_conditions.risk_period: After periods of stress or nutrient deficiency

Tomato_Late_blight.overview: CRITICAL: Same pathogen as Potato Late Blight. Extremely destructive — can wipe out entire crop within days. Requires immediate aggressive treatment.
symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked dark spots on leaves
White mold on leaf undersides in humid conditions
Rapid browning and collapse of leaves
Brown-black stem lesions
Firm brown rot on green fruits
Entire plant can collapse within 7-10 days
causes: Phytophthora infestans spreads via airborne spores
Cool temperatures (10-20°C) with high humidity
Infected transplants from nursery
Spores can travel from neighboring potato fields

immediate_action: URGENT: Apply systemic fungicide within 24 hours
Remove and destroy all infected plant material

prevention: Monitor weather forecasts for blight conditions
Apply preventive fungicide before cool wet periods
Use resistant varieties (Mountain Magic, Defiant)
Avoid planting near potato fields
Remove volunteer tomato and potato plants
Ensure excellent air circulation between plants

treatment_timeline.hour_1_24: URGENT: Apply Ridomil or systemic fungicide NOW
treatment_timeline.day_3: Reassess — if spreading rapidly consider crop destruction
treatment_timeline.week_2: Assess whether crop is worth saving

favorable_conditions.risk_period: Same as potato blight risk periods

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.overview: Leaf mold is primarily a disease of greenhouse and tunnel-grown tomatoes. It thrives in high humidity environments and can cause significant defoliation if left unmanaged.
symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to brown velvety mold on leaf underside
Infected leaves curl upward and wither
Severe cases cause complete defoliation
Flowers and stems rarely affected
causes: Passalora fulva fungus in high humidity (>85%)
Poor ventilation in greenhouses
Temperatures 18-25°C favor disease
Spores persist on surfaces and plant debris
prevention: Maintain humidity below 85% in greenhouses
Space plants for good air circulation
Use resistant varieties (Shirley, Aromata)
Remove lower leaves to improve airflow
Avoid overhead watering
Ventilate greenhouses day and night

treatment_timeline.day_1: Improve ventilation, remove infected leaves, apply fungicide
treatment_timeline.week_1: Monitor humidity levels, reapply if needed
treatment_timeline.week_2_4: Continue spray if humidity stays high

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.overview: Septoria leaf spot is one of the most common tomato diseases, recognizable by its numerous small spots with white/grey centers and dark borders. It rarely kills plants but causes severe defoliation reducing yield.
symptoms: Numerous small circular spots (3-6mm diameter)
Spots have white or grey centers with dark brown borders
Small black dots visible in spot centers (fruiting bodies)
Lower leaves affected first, progresses upward
Severe yellowing and leaf drop
Fruits rarely directly infected
causes: Septoria lycopersici fungus in soil and debris
Spores spread by splashing water
Warm wet weather (20-25°C)
Infection enters through stomata

immediate_action: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash

prevention: 2-3 year crop rotation
Mulch heavily to prevent soil splash
Use drip irrigation
Stake plants to improve airflow
Remove and destroy all crop debris after season
Apply preventive fungicide at first flowering

treatment_timeline.day_1: Remove lower infected leaves, apply Chlorothalonil

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.overview: Spider mites are tiny arachnids (not insects) that feed on plant cells. They thrive in hot dry conditions and can rapidly build up large populations. Recognizable by fine webbing on leaves.
symptoms: Fine webbing on undersides of leaves and between stems
Tiny yellow or white stippling/speckling on leaf surface
Leaves turn bronze, yellow then brown
Tiny moving dots visible on leaf underside (the mites)
Leaves dry out and drop in severe infestations
Plants appear dusty or bronzed overall
causes: Tetranychus urticae — two-spotted spider mite
Hot dry conditions (>30°C, low humidity) favor rapid reproduction
Dusty conditions help spread
Overuse of broad-spectrum insecticides kills natural predators
Stressed plants more susceptible

severity_details.spread_speed: very fast in hot dry conditions

immediate_action: Spray plants forcefully with water to knock off mites

chemical_treatments.name: Abamectin
chemical_treatments.notes: Very effective miticide — rotate to prevent resistance

organic_treatments.name: Neem Oil
organic_treatments.notes: Effective on eggs and young mites

organic_treatments.name: Insecticidal Soap
organic_treatments.notes: Must contact mites directly to work

prevention: Monitor plants weekly — check leaf undersides
Maintain adequate irrigation — mites thrive in drought
Avoid dusty conditions around plants
Conserve natural enemies (ladybugs, predatory mites)
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill beneficial insects
Use reflective mulch to repel mites

treatment_timeline.day_1: Water spray, apply neem oil or abamectin
treatment_timeline.day_5_7: Check population, reapply if still present
treatment_timeline.week_2: Switch miticide class to prevent resistance

Tomato__Target_Spot.overview: Target spot causes circular brown lesions with concentric rings on leaves, stems and fruits. Common in tropical and subtropical regions with high humidity.
symptoms: Circular brown spots with concentric target-like rings
Spots up to 1cm diameter on leaves
Yellow halo around spots
Dark sunken lesions on fruits
Stem cankers in severe cases
causes: Corynespora cassiicola fungus
High humidity and temperatures 20-30°C
Airborne spores land on wet leaf surfaces
Dense plant canopy restricts airflow
prevention: Prune lower leaves for airflow
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply preventive fungicide in humid weather
2-year crop rotation

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.overview: TYLCV is one of the most destructive tomato viruses worldwide. Transmitted by whiteflies, it stunts plants and can cause 100% yield loss if infection occurs early in the season. There is NO cure — focus is on prevention and whitefly control.
symptoms: Upward curling and cupping of young leaves
Leaves turn yellow (chlorotic) especially margins
Severe stunting of plant growth
Small distorted leaves
Flower drop leading to no fruit set
Plants infected early produce no yield
causes: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) — a Begomovirus
Transmitted ONLY by silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)
Whitefly acquires virus in 15-30 minutes of feeding
Can transmit virus for weeks after acquisition
No seed transmission

immediate_action: There is NO cure for virus-infected plants
Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread
Install reflective mulch to repel whiteflies

chemical_treatments.name: Imidacloprid (Whitefly control)
chemical_treatments.notes: Controls whitefly to prevent further spread — does NOT cure virus

organic_treatments.name: Neem Oil
organic_treatments.notes: Repels and disrupts whitefly life cycle

prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties (HM 7882, Shanty)
Use insect-proof netting in nurseries
Install yellow sticky traps at crop edges
Apply imidacloprid at transplanting
Remove infected plants immediately
Control weed hosts of whitefly
Establish whitefly-free nurseries

treatment_timeline.at_detection: Remove infected plants, begin whitefly control
treatment_timeline.ongoing: Monitor and trap whiteflies throughout season
treatment_timeline.next_season: Plant resistant varieties, prepare insect barriers

favorable_conditions.season: Dry season with high whitefly pressure

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.overview: Tomato mosaic virus causes mottled discoloration of leaves and fruit quality reduction. Unlike TYLCV, it spreads by contact (tools, hands) not insects. Extremely stable — can survive on surfaces for years.
symptoms: Mottled light and dark green mosaic pattern on leaves
Leaf distortion and curling
Stunted plant growth
Yellow streaking on stems
Fruits show internal browning (chocolate fruit)
Reduced fruit size and quality
causes: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) spread by contact
Transmitted on hands, tools, clothing
Seed-transmitted (can survive in seed coat)
Extremely stable — survives 2+ years on dry surfaces
Tobacco products can carry related virus (TMV)

immediate_action: No cure exists for infected plants
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution
Wash hands thoroughly before handling plants

organic_treatments.name: Nonfat Dry Milk Solution
organic_treatments.notes: Milk proteins inactivate virus on surfaces

prevention: Use virus-indexed certified seeds
Disinfect tools between plants with bleach
Do not smoke near tomato plants
Wash hands before handling plants
Remove infected plants immediately
Plant resistant varieties (with Tm-2 gene)
Control aphids which may spread secondary infections

treatment_timeline.at_detection: Remove infected plants, sanitize all tools and hands
treatment_timeline.ongoing: Strict sanitation protocol for rest of season
treatment_timeline.next_season: Use virus-free certified seed, resistant varieties

Tomato_healthy.overview: Your tomato plant looks healthy! Tomatoes are susceptible to many diseases — stay vigilant and maintain good practices.

immediate_action: No treatment needed — maintain good practices

prevention: Apply preventive fungicide at first flowering
Water at base of plants only
Stake and prune for good air circulation
Monitor weekly for early disease signs
Rotate crops every 2-3 years
maintenance_tips: Fertilize with calcium-rich fertilizer to prevent blossom end rot
Mulch to maintain soil moisture and prevent splash
Remove suckers weekly for indeterminate varieties
Check undersides of leaves weekly for pests
